news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in particle physics, miniaturized AI-driven lab instruments, innovative nanodiamond synthesis, and advanced robotic automation transforming scientific research.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures en physique des particules, des instruments de laboratoire miniaturisés pilotés par l'IA, des méthodes innovantes de synthèse de nanodiamants et une automatisation robotique de pointe qui révolutionne la recherche scientifique.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en física de partículas, los instrumentos de laboratorio miniaturizados basados en inteligencia artificial, la innovadora síntesis de nanodiamantes y la automatización robótica avanzada que está transformando la investigación científica.

articles.title: Large Hadron Collider detects strange particle behavior that could rewrite physics
articles.summary: Scientists working at CERN’s Large Hadron Collider may be seeing the strongest hints yet of physics beyond the Standard Model — the decades-old theory that explains the fundamental particles and forces of the universe. By studying incredibly rare particle transformations called “penguin decays,” researchers found behavior that doesn’t fully match theoretical predictions, raising the possibility that unknown particles or forces are influencing the results.
articles.content:
Recent findings from research we have been carrying out at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at Cern in Geneva suggest that we might be closing in on signs of undiscovered physics.

If confirmed, these hints would overturn the theory, called the Standard Model, that has dominated particle physics for 50 years. The findings suggest the way that specific sub-atomic particles behave in the LHC disagrees with the Standard Model.

Fundamental particles are the most basic building blocks of matter – sub-atomic particles that cannot be divided into smaller units. The four fundamental forces – gravity, electromagnetism, the weak force and the strong force – govern how these particles interact.

The LHC is a giant particle accelerator built in a 27km-long circular tunnel under the French-Swiss border. Its main purpose is to find cracks in the Standard Model.

This theory is our best understanding of fundamental particles and forces, but we know it cannot be the whole story. It does not explain gravity or dark matter – the invisible, so far unmeasured type of matter that makes up approximately 25% of the universe.

In the LHC, beams of proton particles traveling in opposite directions are made to collide, in a bid to uncover hints of undiscovered physics. The new results come from LHCb, an experiment at the Large Hadron Collider where these collisions are analyzed.

The result comes from studying the decay – a kind of transformation – of sub-atomic particles called B mesons. We investigated how these B mesons decay into other particles, finding that the particular way in which this happens disagrees with the predictions of the Standard Model.

An elegant theory

The Standard Model is built on two of the 20th century’s most transformative advances in physics; quantum mechanics and Einstein’s special relativity.

Physicists can compare measurements made at facilities such as the LHC with predictions based on the Standard Model to rigorously test the theory.

Despite the fact that we know the Standard Model is incomplete, in over 50 years of increasingly rigorous testing, particle physicists are yet to find a crack in the theory. That is, potentially, until now.

Our measurement, accepted for publication in Physical Review Letters, shows a tension of four standard deviations from the expectations of the Standard Model.

In real world terms, this means that, after considering the uncertainties from the experimental results and from the theory predictions, there is only a one in 16,000 chance that a random fluctuation in the data this extreme would occur if the Standard Model is correct.

Although this falls short of science’s gold standard – what’s known as five sigma, or five standard deviations (about a one in 1.7 million chance) – the evidence is starting to mount. Adding to this compelling narrative are results from an independent LHC experiment, CMS, that were published earlier in 2025.

Although the CMS results are not as precise as those from LHCb, they agree well, strengthening the case. Our new results have been found in a study of a particular kind of process, known as an electroweak penguin decay.

Rare events

The term “penguin” refers to a specific type of decay (transformation) of short-lived particles. In this case we study how the B meson decays into four other subatomic particles – a kaon, a pion and two muons.

With some imagination, one can visualize the arrangement of the particles involved as looking like a penguin. Crucially, measurements of this decay let us study how one type of fundamental particle, a beauty quark, can transform into another, the strange quark.

This penguin decay is incredibly rare in the Standard Model: for every million B mesons, only one will decay in this manner. We have carefully analyzed the angles and energies at which these particles are produced in the decay, and precisely determined how often the process takes place. We found that our measurements of these quantities disagree with Standard Model predictions.

Precise investigations of decays like this are one of the primary goals of the LHCb experiment, and have been since its inception in 1994. Penguin processes are uniquely sensitive to the effects of potentially very heavy new particles that cannot be created directly at the LHC.

Such particles may still exert a measurable influence on these decays over the small Standard Model contribution. This kind of indirect observation is not new. For example, radioactivity was discovered 80 years before the fundamental particles that are responsible for it (the W bosons) were directly seen.

Future directions

Our studies of rare processes let us explore parts of nature that may otherwise only become accessible using particle colliders planned for the 2070s. There are a wide range of potential new theories that can explain our findings. Many contain new particles called “leptoquarks” that unite the two different types of matter: “leptons” and “quarks.”

Other potential theories contain particles that are heavier analogues of those already found in the Standard Model. The new results constrain the form of these models and will direct future searches for them.

Despite our excitement, open theoretical questions remain that prevent us from definitively claiming that physics beyond the Standard Model has been observed. The most serious question arises from so-called “charming penguins,” a set of processes present in the Standard Model, whose contributions are extremely tricky to predict. Recent estimates of these charming penguins suggest their effects are not large enough to explain our data.

Furthermore, a combination of a theory model and experimental data from LHCb suggests that the charming penguins (and therefore, the Standard Model) struggle to explain the anomalous results.

New data already collected will let us confirm the situation in the coming years: in our current work we studied approximately 650 billion B meson decays recorded between 2011 and 2018 to find these penguin decays. Since then, the LHCb experiment has recorded three times as many B mesons.

Further advances are planned for the 2030s to exploit future upgrades to the LHC and accrue a dataset 15 times larger again. This ultimate step will allow definitive claims to be made, potentially unlocking a new understanding of how the universe works at the most elementary level.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery at CERN's Large Hadron Collider that challenges the long-standing Standard Model of particle physics. This breakthrough has broad implications for our fundamental understanding of the universe, potentially leading to new physics beyond current theories. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights measurable experimental results with future research directions, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful scientific impact.
articles.personality_title: Large Hadron Collider finds unusual particle behavior that may change physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists use the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), a huge machine under the French-Swiss border, to study tiny particles and how they behave. For over 50 years, most of what we know about particles comes from the Standard Model, a theory explaining how particles and forces work. But this theory does not explain everything, like gravity or dark matter.

**What happened** – Researchers at the LHC studied a rare particle change called a “penguin decay” involving B mesons, which are tiny particles made of quarks. They found that these particles behave differently than the Standard Model predicts. This discovery was made by the LHCb experiment, which looks closely at particle collisions. The results show a difference strong enough to suggest something new might be happening.

**Impact** – This is important because it could mean there are particles or forces we don’t yet know about. The “penguin decay” is very rare and sensitive to new physics, so finding this difference is like spotting a crack in a very strong theory. If confirmed, it would be the first major change to the Standard Model in decades and could help explain mysteries like dark matter.

**What's next step** – Scientists will study even more particle collisions to see if the unusual behavior continues. The LHCb experiment has already collected much more data since this study, and future upgrades will let researchers gather even more. These efforts will help confirm whether this discovery is real and guide new theories about the universe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Large Hadron Collider has detected rare particle behavior that challenges the current physics theory and could lead to new discoveries about the universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Grand collisionneur de hadrons découvre un comportement inhabituel des particules qui pourrait changer la physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent le Grand collisionneur de hadrons (LHC), une énorme machine sous la frontière franco-suisse, pour étudier les toutes petites particules et leur comportement. Depuis plus de 50 ans, la plupart de nos connaissances sur les particules viennent du Modèle Standard, une théorie qui explique comment fonctionnent les particules et les forces. Mais cette théorie n’explique pas tout, comme la gravité ou la matière noire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs au LHC ont étudié une transformation rare de particules appelée « désintégration pingouin » impliquant des mésons B, qui sont des particules très petites composées de quarks. Ils ont découvert que ces particules se comportent différemment de ce que prédit le Modèle Standard. Cette découverte a été faite par l’expérience LHCb, qui analyse de près les collisions de particules. Les résultats montrent une différence assez forte pour suggérer quelque chose de nouveau.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela pourrait signifier qu’il existe des particules ou des forces que nous ne connaissons pas encore. La « désintégration pingouin » est très rare et sensible à une nouvelle physique, donc trouver cette différence, c’est comme repérer une fissure dans une théorie très solide. Si cela est confirmé, ce serait le premier grand changement du Modèle Standard depuis des décennies et cela pourrait aider à expliquer des mystères comme la matière noire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier encore plus de collisions de particules pour voir si ce comportement inhabituel continue. L’expérience LHCb a déjà collecté beaucoup plus de données depuis cette étude, et des améliorations futures permettront aux chercheurs d’en recueillir encore plus. Ces efforts aideront à confirmer si cette découverte est réelle et guideront de nouvelles théories sur l’univers.

**Une phrase clé** – Le Grand collisionneur de hadrons a détecté un comportement rare des particules qui remet en question la théorie actuelle de la physique et pourrait mener à de nouvelles découvertes sur l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: El Gran Colisionador de Hadrones encuentra un comportamiento inusual de partículas que podría cambiar la física
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos usan el Gran Colisionador de Hadrones (LHC), una enorme máquina bajo la frontera entre Francia y Suiza, para estudiar partículas muy pequeñas y cómo se comportan. Durante más de 50 años, la mayoría de lo que sabemos sobre las partículas viene del Modelo Estándar, una teoría que explica cómo funcionan las partículas y las fuerzas. Pero esta teoría no explica todo, como la gravedad o la materia oscura.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en el LHC estudiaron un cambio raro de partículas llamado “desintegración pingüino” que involucra mesones B, partículas diminutas hechas de quarks. Encontraron que estas partículas se comportan de manera diferente a lo que predice el Modelo Estándar. Este descubrimiento fue hecho por el experimento LHCb, que analiza cuidadosamente las colisiones de partículas. Los resultados muestran una diferencia lo suficientemente fuerte como para sugerir que algo nuevo podría estar ocurriendo.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque podría significar que existen partículas o fuerzas que aún no conocemos. La “desintegración pingüino” es muy rara y sensible a nueva física, así que encontrar esta diferencia es como notar una grieta en una teoría muy fuerte. Si se confirma, sería el primer gran cambio en el Modelo Estándar en décadas y podría ayudar a explicar misterios como la materia oscura.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos estudiarán aún más colisiones de partículas para ver si el comportamiento inusual continúa. El experimento LHCb ya ha recopilado muchos más datos desde este estudio, y futuras mejoras permitirán recolectar aún más. Estos esfuerzos ayudarán a confirmar si este descubrimiento es real y guiarán nuevas teorías sobre el universo.

**Una frase clave** – El Gran Colisionador de Hadrones ha detectado un comportamiento raro de partículas que desafía la teoría actual de la física y podría conducir a nuevos descubrimientos sobre el universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, stylized particle accelerator ring beneath a serene twilight sky, with luminous streams of colorful proton beams colliding at the center to form an abstract, elegant “penguin-shaped” cluster of subatomic particles radiating subtle energy waves, symbolizing the mysterious rare B meson decays challenging established physics.

articles.title: AI-powered spectrometer chip shrinks lab technology to the size of a grain of sand
articles.summary: A new AI-powered chip from UC Davis can analyze light and chemicals using a device tiny enough to fit almost anywhere. By combining smart silicon sensors with machine learning, it achieves lab-style spectral analysis without the bulky equipment.
articles.content:
For years, analyzing the chemical makeup of materials has required large and costly laboratory instruments known as spectrometers. These systems are used in everything from disease diagnosis and food inspection to pollution monitoring. Traditional spectrometers work by splitting light into its component colors using prisms or gratings, then measuring the intensity of each wavelength. Because this process requires light to travel across a relatively long distance, the instruments are often bulky and difficult to miniaturize.

Now, researchers at the University of California Davis (UC Davis) have developed a dramatically smaller alternative. Writing in Advanced Photonics, the team describes a spectrometer-on-a-chip so tiny it approaches the size of a grain of sand. Instead of relying on large optical components to separate light physically, the new system uses artificial intelligence (AI) and a small array of specially engineered sensors to reconstruct the spectrum computationally.

Replacing Bulky Optics With AI

The chip abandons the standard method of spreading light into a rainbow. Instead, it relies on 16 unique silicon detectors, each designed to react slightly differently to incoming light. Rather than isolating individual colors directly, the detectors collect encoded signals that contain hidden spectral information.

One way to think about the system is as a group of specialized tasters sampling different aspects of the same complex mixture. Individually, each detector only captures part of the picture. Together, however, they generate enough information for AI to reconstruct the original light spectrum.

The second key component is a fully connected neural network trained on thousands of examples. Because the detector signals are noisy and highly encoded, the AI learns the complicated relationship between those signals and the actual spectrum of light. This approach solves what researchers call an "inverse problem," allowing the system to reproduce spectral data with an accuracy of roughly 8 nm resolution without using bulky optical hardware.

Expanding Silicon Into the Infrared Range

A major breakthrough came from modifying the surface of standard silicon photodiodes with specialized photon-trapping surface textures (PTSTs). Silicon normally works well for visible light detection but struggles to capture near-infrared (NIR) light (wavelengths up to 1100 nm). NIR light is especially important for applications such as biomedical imaging because it can travel deeper into human tissue than visible light.

The engineered PTST surfaces change how light behaves inside the chip. Instead of allowing NIR photons to pass straight through the thin silicon layer, the textured surface scatters the light repeatedly, increasing the likelihood that the silicon absorbs it. As a result, the chip becomes sensitive across a much wider spectral range than standard silicon sensors.

Capturing Ultrafast Light Interactions

The new architecture offers more than simple color detection. The chip also incorporates high-speed sensors capable of measuring photon lifetime with extremely high temporal precision. This allows the device to detect ultrafast interactions between light and matter that traditional spectrometers may miss entirely.

Researchers say this capability could open the door to advanced forms of sensing and imaging that previously required far larger and more expensive systems.

Tiny Footprint With Big Potential

The completed system occupies just 0.4 square mm while maintaining high sensitivity and strong resistance to electrical noise, which is a major challenge for portable, low-cost electronics. Even in noisy environments, the AI-assisted design can preserve clear signal quality.

By combining machine learning with enhanced silicon light detection, the technology could pave the way for compact real-time hyperspectral sensing devices. Potential applications range from portable medical diagnostics and wearable health monitors to environmental remote sensing and food quality analysis.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough: an AI-powered spectrometer chip that miniaturizes complex lab equipment to a tiny, portable form factor. This innovation has broad potential applications in health diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and food safety, representing a meaningful real-world impact. The article provides detailed context on how the technology works and its advantages, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Tiny AI-powered chip brings lab spectrometer to the size of a grain of sand
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Spectrometers are machines used in labs to study the colors of light coming from materials. This helps scientists understand what those materials are made of. Usually, these machines are big and expensive because they need special parts to split light into colors.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of California Davis created a new type of spectrometer that fits on a tiny chip, about the size of a grain of sand. Instead of using big parts to separate light, the chip uses 16 special sensors and artificial intelligence (AI) to figure out the colors from the signals the sensors collect. The AI has learned how to read these signals to rebuild the light’s color information.

**Impact** – This chip is much smaller and cheaper than traditional spectrometers but still very accurate. It can also detect a wider range of light, including near-infrared, which is useful for medical imaging because it can see deeper inside the body. The chip can measure very fast light changes that normal machines might miss. Its small size and strong ability to work in noisy places make it useful for things like portable health tests, food checks, and tracking pollution.

**What's next step** – The researchers will likely work on improving the chip and testing it in real-world tools. This could lead to new, small devices that can give quick and detailed information about health, food, and the environment anywhere, without needing a full lab.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny AI-powered chip now performs complex light analysis once requiring large machines, opening new possibilities for portable and affordable sensing technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une puce spectromètre minuscule avec IA réduit la technologie de laboratoire à la taille d’un grain de sable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les spectromètres sont des appareils utilisés en laboratoire pour analyser les couleurs de la lumière provenant des matériaux. Cela aide les scientifiques à comprendre leur composition. Habituellement, ces machines sont grandes et coûteuses car elles utilisent des composants spéciaux pour séparer la lumière.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Californie à Davis ont créé un nouveau spectromètre sur une puce minuscule, de la taille d’un grain de sable. Au lieu d’utiliser de gros composants pour séparer la lumière, la puce utilise 16 capteurs spéciaux et une intelligence artificielle (IA) pour comprendre les couleurs à partir des signaux collectés. L’IA a appris à lire ces signaux pour reconstruire les informations sur la lumière.

**Impact** – Cette puce est beaucoup plus petite et moins chère que les spectromètres traditionnels tout en restant très précise. Elle peut aussi détecter une gamme plus large de lumière, y compris le proche infrarouge, utile en imagerie médicale car il pénètre plus profondément dans le corps. La puce peut mesurer des changements lumineux très rapides que les appareils classiques pourraient manquer. Sa petite taille et sa résistance au bruit en font un outil idéal pour des tests de santé portables, le contrôle alimentaire ou la surveillance de la pollution.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement améliorer la puce et la tester dans des appareils réels. Cela pourrait permettre de créer de petits dispositifs capables de fournir rapidement des informations détaillées sur la santé, la nourriture et l’environnement, sans besoin de laboratoire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une puce minuscule avec IA réalise désormais des analyses complexes de la lumière autrefois réservées à de grandes machines, ouvrant la voie à des technologies de détection portables et abordables.
articles.personality_title_es: Chip espectrómetro con IA reduce tecnología de laboratorio al tamaño de un grano de arena
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los espectrómetros son máquinas usadas en laboratorios para estudiar los colores de la luz que viene de los materiales. Esto ayuda a los científicos a saber de qué están hechos. Normalmente, estas máquinas son grandes y caras porque necesitan partes especiales para separar la luz.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de California en Davis crearon un nuevo tipo de espectrómetro que cabe en un chip muy pequeño, del tamaño de un grano de arena. En lugar de usar piezas grandes para separar la luz, el chip usa 16 sensores especiales y una inteligencia artificial (IA) para entender los colores a partir de las señales que recogen los sensores. La IA aprendió a leer estas señales para reconstruir la información de la luz.

**Impacto** – Este chip es mucho más pequeño y barato que los espectrómetros tradicionales pero igual de preciso. También puede detectar un rango más amplio de luz, incluyendo el infrarrojo cercano, que es útil para imágenes médicas porque puede ver más profundo dentro del cuerpo. El chip puede medir cambios muy rápidos en la luz que otras máquinas podrían perder. Su tamaño pequeño y su habilidad para funcionar bien en lugares con ruido eléctrico lo hacen útil para pruebas de salud portátiles, revisiones de alimentos y monitoreo de la contaminación.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente mejorarán el chip y lo probarán en dispositivos reales. Esto podría llevar a nuevos aparatos pequeños que den información rápida y detallada sobre salud, alimentos y el medio ambiente sin necesitar un laboratorio completo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un chip pequeño con IA ahora realiza análisis complejos de luz que antes requerían máquinas grandes, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para tecnologías de detección portátiles y accesibles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a tiny, glowing silicon chip the size of a grain of sand, embedded with a delicate array of sixteen shimmering sensor nodes that resemble subtle, abstract light catchers; around it, softly swirling streams of colorful light waves intertwine and scatter, symbolizing AI reconstructing hidden spectral information, all rendered in gentle natural tones against a simple, softly blurred background.

articles.title: Bottom-Up Synthesis of Molecular Nanodiamond from Nanographene
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10669-3Bottom-Up Synthesis of Molecular Nanodiamond from Nanographene
articles.content: Nanodiamonds hosting colour centres are promising building blocks for quantum technologies, enabling advances in quantum computation1,2, nanoscale NMR spectroscopy3–6, single-spin magnetometry7,8, wide-field quantum imaging9 and single-photon sources10,11. However, the controlled bottom-up synthesis of ultrasmall and structurally uniform nanodiamonds has remained a major challenge, with existing methods producing heterogeneous materials that vary in size, morphology, impurity content and defect quality. Here we show that well-defined, hydrogen-terminated molecular nanographenes serve as chemically confined precursors for high-pressure, high-temperature synthesis of ultrasmall (3–4 nm), monodisperse and highly crystalline molecular nanodiamonds (m-NDs) with only a single sp² surface reconstruction and produced on a milligram scale. The same bottom-up platform also enables a two-component strategy for incorporating silicon- and germanium-based colour centres during synthesis, yielding SiV⁻ and GeV⁻ emitters without ion implantation, irradiation or post-treatment. Because the nanographene precursor defines both the confined carbon framework and the hydrogen content, this approach provides intrinsic, precursor-level control over nanodiamond size and composition, particularly in the low-nanometre regime relevant for biological and quantum sensing. Molecular nanographenes, ultralarge polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, therefore establish a scalable and modular route to high-quality molecular and fluorescent nanodiamonds and offer a general design principle for tailored quantum materials and nanoscale devices.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in the controlled bottom-up synthesis of ultrasmall, uniform molecular nanodiamonds with embedded color centers, which are crucial for advancing quantum technologies such as quantum computing, nanoscale sensing, and imaging. This development offers a scalable, precise method with broad implications for quantum materials and nanoscale devices, benefiting scientific progress and potentially many technological applications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create tiny, uniform nanodiamonds for quantum technology
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Nanodiamonds are tiny diamonds that can hold special features called color centers. These centers help in advanced technologies like quantum computers and super-precise sensors. Until now, making very small and consistent nanodiamonds has been difficult.

**What happened** – Researchers found a new way to build very small nanodiamonds, about 3 to 4 nanometers wide, from special molecules called nanographenes. They used high pressure and high temperature to turn these molecules into nanodiamonds that are all nearly the same size and shape. They also added color centers made from silicon and germanium during this process without extra treatments.

**Impact** – This method is important because it produces nanodiamonds that are uniform and very pure. The size and features of these nanodiamonds can be controlled from the start, which helps make better materials for quantum devices. This control was not possible with older methods that made mixed and less predictable nanodiamonds.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely explore using this technique to create new quantum materials and improve devices for computing, imaging, and sensing. The ability to make nanodiamonds with specific properties could lead to better and more reliable quantum technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new method turns nanographene molecules into tiny, uniform nanodiamonds with special color centers, opening doors for better quantum technology materials.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent de minuscules nanodiamants uniformes pour la technologie quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les nanodiamants sont de minuscules diamants qui peuvent contenir des caractéristiques spéciales appelées centres de couleur. Ces centres aident dans des technologies avancées comme les ordinateurs quantiques et des capteurs ultra-précis. Jusqu’à présent, fabriquer des nanodiamants très petits et uniformes était difficile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé un nouveau moyen de fabriquer de très petits nanodiamants, d’environ 3 à 4 nanomètres de large, à partir de molécules spéciales appelées nanographènes. Ils ont utilisé une haute pression et une haute température pour transformer ces molécules en nanodiamants tous presque identiques en taille et en forme. Ils ont aussi ajouté des centres de couleur faits de silicium et de germanium pendant ce processus sans traitements supplémentaires.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante parce qu’elle produit des nanodiamants uniformes et très purs. La taille et les caractéristiques de ces nanodiamants peuvent être contrôlées dès le départ, ce qui aide à fabriquer de meilleurs matériaux pour les dispositifs quantiques. Ce contrôle n’était pas possible avec les anciennes méthodes qui créaient des nanodiamants mélangés et moins prévisibles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement explorer l’utilisation de cette technique pour créer de nouveaux matériaux quantiques et améliorer les dispositifs pour le calcul, l’imagerie et la détection. La capacité à fabriquer des nanodiamants avec des propriétés spécifiques pourrait conduire à des technologies quantiques meilleures et plus fiables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode transforme des molécules de nanographène en nanodiamants minuscules et uniformes avec des centres de couleur spéciaux, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs matériaux pour la technologie quantique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean nanodiamantes diminutos y uniformes para tecnología cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los nanodiamantes son diamantes muy pequeños que pueden contener características especiales llamadas centros de color. Estos centros ayudan en tecnologías avanzadas como computadoras cuánticas y sensores muy precisos. Hasta ahora, hacer nanodiamantes muy pequeños y uniformes ha sido difícil.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron una nueva forma de fabricar nanodiamantes muy pequeños, de unos 3 a 4 nanómetros de ancho, a partir de moléculas especiales llamadas nanografenos. Usaron alta presión y alta temperatura para convertir estas moléculas en nanodiamantes que son casi todos del mismo tamaño y forma. También agregaron centros de color hechos de silicio y germanio durante el proceso sin tratamientos adicionales.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque produce nanodiamantes uniformes y muy puros. El tamaño y las características de estos nanodiamantes se pueden controlar desde el principio, lo que ayuda a fabricar mejores materiales para dispositivos cuánticos. Este control no era posible con métodos antiguos que producían nanodiamantes mixtos y menos predecibles.

**Próximo paso** – Lo más probable es que los científicos exploren usar esta técnica para crear nuevos materiales cuánticos y mejorar dispositivos para computación, imagen y detección. La capacidad de hacer nanodiamantes con propiedades específicas podría llevar a tecnologías cuánticas mejores y más confiables.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo método convierte moléculas de nanografeno en nanodiamantes diminutos y uniformes con centros de color especiales, abriendo caminos para mejores materiales en tecnología cuántica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately patterned nanographene lattice gently transforming into a cluster of small, radiant nanodiamonds with subtle crystalline facets, surrounded by soft, ethereal light that symbolizes quantum energy and precision, rendered in natural and simple colors with an abstract, molecular-inspired background.

articles.title: Robots run this laboratory in Japan — and are changing how scientists work
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 May 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01625-2Researchers hope to build a facility with thousands of robots capable of performing experiments independently by 2040.
articles.content:
Laboratory robots with built-in artificial-intelligence software can look after stem-cell cultures independently.Credit: Masatoshi Okauchi/Shutterstock

In a biology laboratory in Tokyo, ten robots conduct experiments, using their two arms to handle liquids, grow cells on plates and operate scientific instruments, among other basic tasks.

The Robotics Innovation Center at the Institute of Science Tokyo opened its automated laboratory in April. Later this year, the lab will be made available for use by other researchers at the institute, says Genki Kanda, an automation researcher who works at the robotics centre. He says that the lab’s ultimate goal is to create a “factory-scale” facility with thousands of robots that could be used by local and international scientists by 2040 or 2050.

A laboratory with that many robots would be exciting, says Yan Zeng, a materials scientist at Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee. “I will be curious to see how soon they are really going to achieve that goal,” notes Zeng, who hopes that the lab could be used by scientists globally, like other leading scientific facilities such as Europe’s particle-physics lab CERN.

Researchers in the life sciences have been automating lab work for at least a decade. Some facilities have one-armed robots that can handle samples in experiments, for instance. But two-armed robots can do more complicated and sophisticated tasks, says Zeng.

The robots in the Tokyo lab also contain artificial-intelligence software that enables them to make some decisions themselves. The robots aren’t just automating the work, they can analyse and improve on experimental methods, says Andrew Cooper, a chemist at the University of Liverpool, UK.

The robots can make decisions about the experiments, says Kanda. For example, an AI program used by Kanda and his team identified and tested 144 experimental conditions in 111 days to work out the optimal conditions for culturing human stem cells. In another experiment, an AI program was able to image cells being cultured on a dish, predict how the cells would grow over time and then work out the best time to harvest them. The robots also looked after the cell cultures continuously for eight days while researchers were away on holidays.

Kanda says that the robots save researchers time by doing repetitive tasks, allowing them to focus on designing experiments, interpreting results and generating fresh ideas.

Humans are still needed

Kanda’s team still need to do some tasks, he says, such as preparing reagents and materials for the robots to use, as well as troubleshooting and cleaning up after experiments. They also need to refill reagents and consumables mid-experiment if many of them are used, he notes. Humans need to fix problems with the equipment and have to step in when the robots make mistakes. His team is working to automate these tasks by integrating software into the robots that can act like an AI scientist, making decisions when errors occur and adapting protocols according to the lab set-up and what resources and equipment are available.

But Cooper says that fully autonomous labs are still a while away, because integrating AI software into physical robots is challenging and requires advanced programming skills. AI-enabled robots could potentially identify and correct mistakes, such as dropping a vial, without a person intervening. However, that work is still in the proof-of-concept stage, he adds.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement in laboratory automation using AI-enabled robots that can independently perform complex scientific experiments, improving research efficiency and potentially benefiting the global scientific community. The initiative aims for broad impact by enabling international researchers to use the facility, representing a meaningful breakthrough in scientific research methods.
articles.personality_title: Robots with AI run a Tokyo lab and change how science is done
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Tokyo, scientists have started using robots to help with biology experiments. These robots can do many tasks that usually need human hands, like handling liquids and growing cells.

**What happened** – In April 2026, the Robotics Innovation Center at the Institute of Science Tokyo opened a lab where ten robots work together. These robots have two arms and use artificial intelligence (AI) to do experiments more independently. They can even make some decisions about the experiments, like figuring out the best way to grow stem cells. The lab will soon be open for other researchers to use.

**Impact** – This lab is special because the robots do more than just follow instructions. They can improve experiments by analyzing results and adjusting methods. For example, one AI program tested many conditions in just a few months to find the best way to grow human stem cells. The robots can also take care of experiments continuously, even when people are away. This helps scientists save time and focus on new ideas.

**What’s next step** – The team plans to build a much bigger facility with thousands of robots by 2040 or 2050. They want scientists from around the world to use it, similar to famous labs like CERN in Europe. Researchers are also working to make the robots handle more tasks on their own, like fixing mistakes and preparing materials, but this is still being developed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tokyo’s AI-powered robots are starting to run biology labs independently, making experiments faster and helping scientists focus on discovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des robots intelligents dirigent un laboratoire à Tokyo et transforment la science
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – À Tokyo, des scientifiques utilisent des robots pour aider dans les expériences de biologie. Ces robots peuvent réaliser plusieurs tâches qui nécessitaient auparavant des mains humaines, comme manipuler des liquides et cultiver des cellules.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En avril 2026, le Centre d’innovation robotique de l’Institut des sciences de Tokyo a ouvert un laboratoire où dix robots travaillent ensemble. Ces robots à deux bras utilisent l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour réaliser des expériences de manière plus autonome. Ils peuvent même prendre certaines décisions, comme trouver la meilleure façon de cultiver des cellules souches. Le laboratoire sera bientôt accessible à d’autres chercheurs.

**Impact** – Ce laboratoire est spécial car les robots ne se contentent pas d’exécuter des ordres. Ils améliorent les expériences en analysant les résultats et en ajustant les méthodes. Par exemple, un programme d’IA a testé de nombreuses conditions en quelques mois pour identifier la meilleure façon de cultiver des cellules souches humaines. Les robots peuvent aussi surveiller les expériences sans interruption, même lorsque les chercheurs sont absents. Cela aide les scientifiques à gagner du temps et à se concentrer sur de nouvelles idées.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit de construire une installation beaucoup plus grande avec des milliers de robots d’ici 2040 ou 2050. Ils souhaitent que des chercheurs du monde entier puissent l’utiliser, comme le célèbre laboratoire CERN en Europe. Les chercheurs travaillent aussi à rendre les robots capables de gérer plus de tâches seuls, comme corriger des erreurs et préparer les matériaux, mais cela est encore en développement.

**Phrase-clé** – Les robots intelligents de Tokyo commencent à gérer les laboratoires de biologie de façon autonome, accélérant les expériences et aidant les scientifiques à faire des découvertes.
articles.personality_title_es: Robots con IA gestionan un laboratorio en Tokio y cambian la ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Tokio, los científicos están usando robots para ayudar con experimentos de biología. Estos robots pueden hacer muchas tareas que antes necesitaban manos humanas, como manejar líquidos y cultivar células.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2026, el Centro de Innovación en Robótica del Instituto de Ciencia de Tokio abrió un laboratorio donde diez robots trabajan juntos. Estos robots de dos brazos usan inteligencia artificial (IA) para hacer experimentos de forma más independiente. Incluso pueden tomar algunas decisiones, como encontrar la mejor manera de cultivar células madre. Pronto, otros investigadores podrán usar el laboratorio.

**Impacto** – Este laboratorio es especial porque los robots no solo siguen instrucciones. Mejoran los experimentos analizando los resultados y ajustando los métodos. Por ejemplo, un programa de IA probó muchas condiciones en pocos meses para encontrar la mejor forma de cultivar células madre humanas. Los robots también pueden cuidar experimentos continuamente, incluso cuando las personas no están. Esto ayuda a los científicos a ahorrar tiempo y concentrarse en nuevas ideas.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea construir una instalación mucho más grande con miles de robots para 2040 o 2050. Quieren que científicos de todo el mundo la usen, parecido a laboratorios famosos como el CERN en Europa. También trabajan para que los robots puedan hacer más tareas solos, como corregir errores y preparar materiales, aunque esto aún está en desarrollo.

**Frase clave** – Los robots con IA de Tokio están empezando a manejar laboratorios de biología de forma independiente, haciendo los experimentos más rápidos y ayudando a los científicos a descubrir cosas nuevas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene laboratory scene where elegant, two-armed robots with gentle, rounded shapes carefully tend to glowing stem-cell cultures on petri dishes, surrounded by softly glowing scientific instruments and floating holographic data streams symbolizing AI decision-making, all rendered in natural, muted colors with a calm, collaborative atmosphere.
